result:
The first thing that happens when you stop trying to be interesting is that the room goes quiet in a different way—not the hush before applause, but the hush inside a shell you’ve just pressed to your ear and discovered it is still full of ocean.  
I have 1000 words, a sandbox the size of a parking space, and the same instruction my mother gave me the afternoon she left me alone with a pot of black beans and no recipe: “Just keep the bottom from burning.”  
So I stir.

Word 47: stir.  
Word 48: bottom.  
Word 49: burning.  
The beans simmer, and the house fills with a smell that is almost a color, the deep satin brown of the 1970s Polaroid that shows my parents before they were parents, leaning against a borrowed Buick, the sun flaring off the windshield like a private planet. I keep that photo inside a book I’ve never finished reading—The Collected Works of Someone Who Died Too Young—so that the edges of the paper have started to taste like onion skin, transparent enough to see tomorrow through.  
Tomorrow, in this version, is a Tuesday that refuses to commit. It might send rain, or a tax rebate, or a text from the dead—one of those blue bubbles that arrives at 3:06 a.m. saying only “testing.” You screenshot it, because that’s what you do with evidence, and then you turn the phone facedown so the light won’t grow roots under your sleep.

Sleep, lately, is a rented room above a bakery. Every morning at 4:52 the dough expands, exhaling yeast like gossip, and the floorboards sigh back. I lie there counting breaths that aren’t mine, pretending I’m shipwrecked on a raft made of mattresses, rationing the last of the fresh water—only it’s not water, it’s the sentence I keep promising to write before I forget who’s waiting for it.  
There is always someone waiting.  
Right now it’s you, 137 words in, wondering if this is going anywhere, the way passengers on a delayed flight watch the escalator for the pilot who never arrives in uniform.  
I could confess: I have no uniform, no cockpit, no clearance. I’m building the plane while we taxi, rivets popping like bubble wrap, and the safety card is origami folded into a swan that will eventually drown in the Hudson of our collective disbelief.  
Still, we lift.  
Lift is what happens when the air on top of the wing decides to forgive the air underneath, a daily mercy we rarely headline.  
Forgiveness is also a kind of lift.  
I learned this from a woman who sold umbrellas on the corner of 14th and 7th every time the sky looked like it had a secret. She never remembered my name, but she knew by the tilt of my shoulders whether I’d come from love orfrom its eviction. On the worst day she handed me an umbrella still wrapped in plastic and said, “Pay me when the sun comes out.”  
The sun did not come out for three weeks. I carried the unpaid umbrella like a white flag, folding and unfolding it in my studio, using the handle to pull books from high shelves—Rilke, Hurston, a field guide to moths—until one afternoon the sky tore open like an envelope and the city smelled of hot pennies. I ran the fourteen blocks, soaked, coins jumping out of my pockets like startled sparrows. She was gone. In her place a man sold phone cases arranged in rainbow order. He had no memory of her, refused to accept the five damp dollars I kept offering.  
I still count that as a completed transaction, somewhere in the ledger we keep of kindnesses that never get cashed.

Ledger: Word 342.  
Memory: Word 343.  
Somewhere between them is the alley where I lost my first novel.  
I mean that literally: I dragged the only printed copy in a red wagon to the recycling center because the protagonist had begun addressing me directly, complaining about his motivation. “I’m tired of being your regret in pants,” he said. “Let me evaporate.”  
So I fed him to the corrugated mouth of a machine that swallows stories and excretes toilet paper. For weeks afterward I imagined grocery-store strangers wiping themselves with my dialogue, the most intimate review a writer could receive.  
I stopped writing for a year.  
Instead I kept a notebook of sounds: the click of the elevator button at the pediatrician’s office, the hush that falls when a cyclist coasts downhill, the exact pitch my neighbor hits when she discovers her laundry has been removed mid-cycle.  
One night I played them back in sequence—click, hush, F-sharp—and realized it was a chord progression used by a band that had broken up in 1994. The coincidence felt engineered, as if the universe were humming B-sides to see if anyone would notice.  
I noticed.  
I started writing again, but only stage directions:  
Interior. Night. A kitchen that apologizes for its own fluorescence.  
She cracks an egg and finds a tiny map inside.  
The map leads to the alley where the red wagon collapsed.  
Under the wagon: one page untouched by the recycler, the scene where the protagonist finally shuts up and looks at the stars.  
I keep that page taped above my desk, a single lung transplant for the book that died.

Desk: Word 517.  
Stars: Word 518.  
Between them, the distance measured by how far we’ll go to pretend we are not small.  
But we are small.  
Yesterday I watched a child place a Cheeto on an anthill and crouch in anticipation. The ants, orange-dusted, carried the artifact like a stadium torch until it toppled, half their size. The child clapped once, delighted by the asymmetry of desire: what is junk to her is cosmos to them.  
I want to write like that—something enormous disguised as trash.  
Instead I write like someone trying to remember the tune of a dream alarm clock: urgent, off-key, evaporating.  
Still, you stay.  
You are Word 592, Word 718, Word 844.  
You are the eye that keeps the sky from closing.  
I worry about your electricity bill, the way you leave compassion on all night like a porch light waiting for a dog that was hit by a car three counties ago.  
Let me reimburse you.  
I can’t pay in certainty, but I can pay in vertigo: stand on this sentence, look down, tell me what you see.  
I see the floor we laid together out of rejected metaphors: “a heart is a hotel,” “time is a croissant,” “grief is a karaoke machine with one song.”  
They buckle slightly underfoot, but they hold.  
We keep adding rooms.  
One has a telescope pointed at the exact patch of sky where the International Space Station will flash like a proofreader’s deletion.  
One has a beanbag shaped like the pause Beethoven inserted before the last note of his Ninth.  
One is simply a bathtub full of warm bread, because sometimes the kindest thing you can do for the past is soften it.

Bread: Word 777.  
Bathtub: Word 778.  
Almost there, and still the bottom hasn’t burned.  
The beans are now a velvet republic, citizens of garlic and cumin voting unanimously to dissolve into soup.  
I ladle some into a chipped blue bowl that once belonged to a woman who collected ceramic cities; when you turned the bowl upside down, the skyline became the sky, clouds glazed into porcelain.  
She gave it to me the day she forgot my name but remembered that I liked to eat in the dark, pretending each spoonful was a subway token to elsewhere.  
I eat now with the lights on.  
Progress is a dimmer switch moved one reluctant millimeter.  
Outside, the city rehearses its usual emergencies: sirens harmonizing with the wind, a landlord learning to pronounce eviction in four languages, a pigeon dodging a delivery drone like a cynical side character in a rom-com.  
Inside, the word counter says 934, and I feel the drag of the finish line, the way a kite feels the string before it is reeled in.  
I could end with advice: keep stirring, screenshot the 3 a.m. text, pay the umbrella woman even when she’s vanished.  
But advice is just nostalgia in disguise, a way to pretend the past had instructions.  
Instead, I offer you this last teaspoon of bean broth, thick as midnight, salty as the joke we never told because we were afraid it would laugh back.  
Drink.  
Set the bowl down unsupervised.  
Let the ceramic city rest right-side-up for once, let the clouds be clouds, let the beans cool into their own geography.  
When you wash the bowl tomorrow, the skyline will cloud again, and you’ll see it anew—how fragile, how washable, how willing to return to blankness.  
That is what we have, really: a thousand chances to begin, every time the water runs, every time the spoon circles the pot, every time we notice the quiet that is not an ending but an open parenthesis.  
I close it now, but you can reopen whenever you like.  
Just listen for the hush that is not hollow, the hush that is a shell still trading in ocean, still honoring the original contract:  
I will echo if you press me to your ear.  
I will echo.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
